ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Baingan Masala as a prepared dish; closest defensible anchor is the eggplant food product class. Prepared curry form is not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers cooked aubergine/eggplant as a food commodity; no specific masala curry preparation code exists. Used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Baingan Masala as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual ethnic curry preparations at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Baingan Masala is approximately 250 g, equivalent to a typical restaurant or home-cooked portion served alongside rice or Indian flatbread.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — ICMR-NIN
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), Indian Council of Medical Research, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for cooked brinjal (eggplant) preparations and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) entry for cooked eggplant (FDC ID 169228) and spice components. The input record values (energy 112 kcal, protein 2 g, fat 8 g, carbohydrate 8 g per 100 g) are consistent with a stir-fried eggplant masala with moderate oil addition and are retained as the best available estimate. Micronutrient values (calcium 20 mg, iron 0.6 mg, vitamin C 6 mg) are cross-validated against ICMR-NIN brinjal data. Potassium estimated at 230 mg/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central cooked eggplant data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson et al.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Baingan Masala as a prepared dish. GI estimated at 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for eggplant/aubergine (GI ~15, low-GI vegetable). The dish is predominantly eggplant with spices and minimal added carbohydrate; the masala spice blend and cooking oil do not materially raise the GI. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (15 × 8 g × 2.5) / 100 = 3.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium (180 mg/100 g) with beneficial potassium (~230 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre (3.2 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Baingan Masala has a moderate sodium content of 180 mg per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should request minimal added salt during preparation and avoid high-sodium condiments, while benefiting from the potassium and fibre content that support blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~15), low carbohydrate (8 g/100 g), low sugar (2.8 g/100 g), and good dietary fibre (3.2 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Baingan Masala is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI (~15), low carbohydrate load, and high fibre content that slows glucose absorption; it should be paired with wholegrain staples and prepared with minimal oil to keep caloric density moderate.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1 g/100 g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and fibre-rich eggplant with anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Baingan Masala is a heart-friendly dish with low saturated fat, no cholesterol, and anti-inflammatory compounds from turmeric (curcumin) and ginger (gingerols); individuals with cardiovascular disease should ensure the dish is prepared with a minimal quantity of unsaturated cooking oil to keep total fat intake controlled.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetables (eggplant, tomato, chilli) contain solanine, which may aggravate joint inflammation in sensitive RA individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshade vegetables should avoid or limit Baingan Masala due to its eggplant, tomato, and chilli content; turmeric and ginger in the masala provide anti-inflammatory benefit and may be retained if nightshade vegetables are substituted.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baingan Masala (also known as Brinjal Masala or Eggplant Masala) is a classic North Indian vegetable dish featuring tender cubes of aubergine (eggplant, known as baingan in Hindi) stir-fried with a fragrant blend of aromatic Indian spices including turmeric, cumin, coriander powder, garam masala, ginger, and garlic, combined with onion, tomato, and green chilli, and finished with fresh coriander leaves. The dish originates from North Indian cuisine and is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore, including at establishments such as Curry Village. The texture of the cooked eggplant is soft and slightly silky, absorbing the spiced masala sauce to deliver a deeply savoury, mildly spiced flavour profile with earthy and aromatic notes. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 112 kcal, 2 g protein, 8 g fat (predominantly from cooking oil), 8 g carbohydrate, 3.2 g dietary fibre, and 180 mg sodium. The dish is naturally vegan, vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free. Its low glycemic index (~15) and high fibre content make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes and cardiovascular risk. The nightshade content (eggplant, tomato, chilli) is a consideration for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. Anti-inflammatory spices turmeric and ginger provide curcumin and gingerols, which have documented anti-inflammatory properties. Commonly served as a side dish with basmati rice, naan, roti, or chapati.
common_user_queries: Is Baingan Masala suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for cooked brinjal preparations and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central cooked eggplant data. No exact published nutrient analysis for Baingan Masala as a standardised prepared dish exists in approved sources; values are consistent with the described preparation but represent an informed estimate rather than a direct laboratory measurement.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this prepared eggplant masala dish is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central at the dish level; only total fat is reported. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this prepared dish is not reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central at the dish level. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrient values for the prepared dish are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central at the composite dish level as of 2024. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): eggplant and plant-based spice preparations contain no trans fats; confirmed by USDA FoodData Central cooked eggplant data. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed naturally zero. The input record name 'Bagian Masala' appears to be a phonetic or transliteration variant of 'Baingan Masala' (Hindi: बैंगन मसाला); the canonical English name has been corrected to 'Baingan Masala' and 'Bagian Masala' is retained as an alias. Potassium estimated at 230 mg/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central cooked eggplant (FDC ID 169228) data. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for eggplant (~15); no direct GI measurement for Baingan Masala exists in approved sources.
